So welcome to AI Engineering Fundamentals.
I'm super excited to teach this class.
This was a class that was kind of dreamt up
over the past year because as a faculty,
we kind of looked at the classes that we were teaching
and we thought about what do our students need to know
to be effective contributors in this kind of AI economy.
And it was determined that the information
that we're gonna put together in this class
wasn't finding its way to students
in all of the other classes.
It's easy to imagine why,
because AI kind of jumped on the scene
and everybody in every industry is reacting to it.
And education is no different than any other industry.
So we had to kind of watch and see how things played out
as AI came on the scene and we observed some patterns.
And one of the big patterns that I've observed
is people leveraging LLMs to build applications on top of.
And that's a major trend these days.
And actually it's what I would say
is the primary source of innovation
because there's only a few companies that can build the LLMs
because pre-training is incredibly expensive.
You have to buy the hardware and you have to do the training
and the training takes a long time
on very expensive hardware.
And so there's a handful of companies
that are building foundational and frontier models.
We know most of those company names,
Microsoft, Google, OpenAI, Claude, Anthropic.
And so there's a smaller set of groups
that are building the foundational models,
but there's many, many, many companies
building on top of those models,
taking advantage of the capabilities
that those models deliver to us.
So that's really what this course is all about

such that when you go into the marketplace
to find a job or start your own company,
as we chatted about,
that you'll know all of the concepts

So the first week of class
is gonna be focused on some of the most important
foundational conceptual understanding
as it relates to AI and LLMs specifically.
This class doesn't talk too much about machine learning.
It doesn't do a deep dive into neural networks.
It kind of touches on them a little bit,
but the class is really focused
on understanding how LLMs work.
And of course, LLMs are taking advantage of neural networks,
but it's really focused on kind of AI
from an LLM perspective.
And as I stated earlier about understanding how that works
so people can build applications
that take advantage of this powerful capability.
So the first thing that I wanna talk about today
is kind of how did we get to where we are in terms of AI?
In terms of LLM, it kind of blew up basically
when ChatGPT presented at Disrupt Conference
a few years ago and started talking about the availability
of their newest model.
But the AI research goes back decades
and Carnegie Mellon was actually a big contributor
to much of this research.
And I wanna just kind of touch on some of the milestones
in the journey so that we're all grounded on them
and we're familiar with kind of how we got
to where we are today
and what capabilities made it possible.
So early attempts at AI were really focused
on what we refer to as rules-based systems.
And this is basically, conceptually,
it's about humans trying to tell machines how to think.
It's a bunch of if-then statements
that we attempt to take our knowledge
and put it into the machine.
Now that only gets you so far for a number of reasons.
Number one, it's brittle.
If there's an outlier, if there's a question that is asked
that you didn't really already tell it
what the answer would be, then it's gonna break.
And it always, it can't really learn on its own, right?
It's dependent on a human to kind of tell it the answer
so that it can then regurgitate the answers.
And we often tried to style teaching machines
after how we styled teaching ourselves.
So as Omri stated out, education has often relied
on kind of one person, the teacher,
to tell the class the truth.
And then the class kind of memorize it
and combines it with some readings
and some homework and some exercises.
And then the class knows what the teacher knows, right?
So it's a very supervised transfer of knowledge.
And so it's very understandable
why early researchers thought that that was the technique
that needed to happen to enable machine learning.
But again, it only got us so far.
And then we kind of went through this phase
where we started kind of learning patterns from data, right?
And this produced some very useful things,
spam filters, recommendation engines.
If you've ever used Netflix, right?
We like it when it thinks it knows us, right?
Because it's looking at what we've watched
and what we've liked in the past
and it can recommend things.
And kind of this machine learning
kind of took us down the road a bit further as well
in the world of AI.
And it was heavily under the hood
relying on statistical models.
Now, neural networks really started
kind of opening the floodgates to really with the hope
that machines could learn on their own.
And there were a number of things happening
during this process.
Number one was the availability of data
was becoming large, right?
As well, the processing power of chips
was becoming more and more powerful.
So we had more data and we had powerful chips
that could do more with that data.
And in this kind of early,
still early phase of kind of neural networks,
we were able to have machines learn on their own,
which was very, very powerful.
And because we basically realized
that humans are not able to hard code the machines
with all the permutations
of potentially what could happen.
And the other kind of learning element
that became evident in this phase
was this concept of prediction, right?
So we've got kind of weights inside of a neural network.
And if we give it a corpus of information,
it can read on its own
and it can predict the next word in the sentence.
And if it gets it wrong,
then it reveals what the right word was.
And then it thinks for a sec, okay, I got it wrong.
So that's a binary.
Was it right or was it wrong?
I got it wrong.
Okay, how far off was I?
That's measuring the loss.
And then gradient descent is pointing the machine
in the right direction to try and be more right
or less wrong the next time around, okay?
So this allowed learning to become kind of like a game
that the machine could play with itself
over and over and over again.
And this was one of the big breakthroughs
because when humans are kind of like
trying to teach the machine the truth as we know it,
it's not this game that can be played 24 seven, okay?
So with processors, with advanced processors,
with a large corpus of information,
the machines can play this game by themselves 24 seven
all day long and constantly expose their model
and then measure how far off they were.
Gradient descent, again, tells us it's kind of like
if you ever play the game kind of warm or cold,
like I'm thinking of a word and you guess,
I'm like, you're cold, right?
Okay, you guess again.
And then I might say you're warm.
Okay, good, I'm going in the right direction.
That kind of thing.
And so then this became a very powerful thing
that the machines could kind of learn on their own.
And ultimately it's about predicting the next word.
I mean, that's kind of what LLMs do
is they just predict the next word in a sentence,
but they do it so well, it appears that they're reasoning.
It appears that they have logic, right?
Because the language that it studied had reasoning
and had logic.
So when they get so good at reproducing,
it appears that they have the same capability,
even though it's not really what's happening
under the hood.
And then there was a big breakthrough
in the transformer architecture.
And this allowed the training to move faster
and the machines to have more contextual knowledge
about the sentences that they were reading
and they were understanding.
So prior to transformer,
it was very sequential understanding of sentences.
With the transformer architecture,
machines could basically read the whole sentence by itself
and each word could give context forwards and backwards.
So a very rich contextual understanding
of what is being stated in the sentence.
And this was the big kind of breakthrough
with the transformer architecture.
And this kind of gave way to large language models
as we know them today,
that we've been living with over the past few years.
Emergent capabilities is what we just talked about
a minute ago.
That's the appearance of reasoning,
the appearance of logic,
the appearance of almost consciousness.
That is emergent capabilities.
And we know that the machines don't have that ability.
They're not conscious, of course,
but they appear that way because they've gotten so good
at reproducing the patterns of words and sentences
and from all the material that they've been trained on,
which is basically everything that's ever been written
in the history of the world,
which is crazy to think about.
Okay, and then we have, you know, Gen AI,
which is systems that can create new content.
And that's really cool because whatever industry we look at,
they're doing amazing things.
Software engineering, right?
It's writing code.
In the medical field, it's giving diagnosis.
In the educational world,

it's also doing really, really cool things.

So there's many layers to neural networks.
There's input, there's output,
and then there's these hidden layers.
And again, the readings are gonna go
really deep into this.
The thing that I always think is so interesting
about LLMs is machines do not understand words.
So we need to take words and turn it into numbers
so that the machine can understand numbers
and then feed us back words.
So it's a very interesting game being played,
words being turned into numbers,
processing inside, coming back out,
being turned back into words that we can understand.
So within the transformer model,
we've got basically input tokens.
Tokens take words, break them down into subparts.
And then we kind of use those subparts
to assign numbers to.
The numbers become embeddings,
which exist inside of the model
in what is often referred to as an n-dimensional space.
I like to think of it like a 3D space,
but much more complicated than three dimensions.
As humans, it's kind of hard for us to visualize
more than three dimensions.
We can visualize space and where things exist in space,
but in an n-dimensional model,
it's actually very difficult to conceptualize
what that looks like.
But that's what's happening in the models.
And then they can kind of measure distances
between words and phrases from each other.
They can understand context and relevance
and do amazing things with it.
So again, these are some of the concepts
that we're going to cover more deeply in our readings.
And I can make this page available to you guys as well,
if that's helpful for you to go into more details.
Tokenization, I think, is fascinating.
There's different tools out there to do tokenization,
but again, that's taking words, breaking them down,
and then turning them into numbers.
And then this is a great example of kind of words
in this n-dimensional space.
So it's got man, woman,
which are kind of close together in the space
because they're two versions of a human.
You've got king and queen, which are close together.
And then you can do these odd things
where you say king minus man plus woman equals queen,
which is conceptually, I think,
very interesting to think about.
This is kind of how the machines
are thinking about these words in the space
and how they make sense of each other.
Training, we talked a little bit about.
We take a massive text corpus.
We do tokenization,
and there's different tools that we can utilize for this.
We do training, right?
It's often referred to as pre-training.
This is very expensive,
and that's why only the biggest companies are doing this.
It takes very expensive hardware.
It takes a lot of time, money, and energy.
And in the training process,
basically weights are being adjusted
over and over and over again
till we reach a point of convergence,
and convergence is the point in time
at which further training doesn't really help
the weights adjust in a meaningful way,
and that's when we can say we're done
with our training run.
Then we've got a pre-trained model,
and then we can do kind of like post-training
model adaptation.
